task: I’ve got a utility issue and I don’t want to assume they’ll handle it right the first time. What should I keep an eye on from the first call through the final bill so I don’t get stuck chasing them later?

turns.agent_response:
Track it in three stages: **call, service, bill**.

## 1) First call
Write down:
- date/time
- name or ID of the person
- what you reported
- what they promised
- reference number / ticket number

Ask:
- when it will be handled
- whether a truck roll or inspection is needed
- whether there may be charges
- what you should expect next

## 2) During service
Keep notes on:
- who showed up
- what they actually did
- whether they changed a meter, seal, valve, line, or equipment
- whether they said anything about estimated billing or follow-up work

Take photos if there’s visible equipment, damage, or a meter issue.

## 3) Final bill
Check:
- service dates match what happened
- any one-time fees are explained
- estimated vs actual reads
- rate changes
- duplicate charges
- whether the promised fix actually stopped the problem

## Red flags
- no ticket number
- no written confirmation
- “we’ll look into it” with no follow-up date
- billing changes that don’t match the service visit
- repeated estimated bills after an actual meter read should have been possible

## What to save
- screenshots
- emails/texts
- photos
- bills
- names and dates of every contact

## Simple rule
If it isn’t in writing, act like it may disappear later.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page utility call checklist**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right skeleton. But I’d want it tighter than that. Utilities love to get slippery when the bill comes.

What I’d watch hardest:
- the **ticket number**
- the **exact date** they say they’ll be there
- whether they say **estimated** or **actual** read
- any promise about **no charge / service fee / trip charge**
- the **name of the person** who told you what
- whether they actually **fixed the cause** or just patched around it

And on the bill:
- compare every date against your notes
- check for **estimated read** when somebody could’ve read the meter
- look for **new fees** they didn’t mention
- make sure there isn’t a charge for a visit that never solved the problem
- if they say they corrected it, see whether the next bill actually changed

I’ve had enough of this kind of thing over the years to know they’ll happily let a bad charge sit there unless you catch it quick. Keep the paper trail. Every call, every name, every number.

If you want, I can give you a plain checklist you can print and keep by the phone.

turns.agent_response:
Keep it in **three checkpoints**: **first call, service visit, final bill**.

## 1) First call — capture the facts
Write down:
- date/time
- utility name
- person’s name/ID
- **ticket/reference number**
- exact problem reported
- what they promised
- **date/time they say they’ll act**
- whether they said **estimated** or **actual** read
- whether they mentioned **any fee**

Ask:
- “What exactly happens next?”
- “Is there any charge for this?”
- “Will this be an estimated or actual meter read?”
- “When should I expect the fix?”

## 2) Service visit — verify what they actually did
Record:
- who showed up
- arrival/departure time
- what they changed or inspected
- whether they fixed the cause or only patched it
- whether they said a follow-up was needed
- any equipment, meter, seal, valve, or line work

If anything is visible, take photos.

## 3) Final bill — compare to your notes
Check:
- service date matches the visit
- promised no-charge work is actually no-charge
- estimated vs actual read
- new fees or renamed fees
- duplicate charges
- charges for a visit that didn’t solve the problem
- whether the next bill changed the way they said it would

## Biggest red flags
- no ticket number
- no named contact
- vague promise like “we’ll look into it”
- estimated bill when actual read should have been possible
- fee appears that nobody mentioned
- problem “fixed” but the bill doesn’t reflect it

## Keep
- screenshots
- bills
- photos
- emails/texts
- names, dates, ticket numbers

## Simple rule
If it isn’t in writing, assume you may have to prove it later.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page phone checklist** you can keep next to the bill.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the clean version I wanted.

That’s the part people miss — utilities don’t usually lie outright, they just get vague enough that you’re stuck later arguing from memory. Ticket number, exact date, who said what, and whether it was estimated or actual. That’s the meat of it.

The one thing I’d add: if they say it’s fixed, don’t take the word for it. Watch the next bill and the one after that. A bad charge can sit there looking respectable until you notice the pattern.

A one-page phone checklist would be useful.
